document.critique: I checked machine counts, all separation/sameness rules, both exact-one constraints, and conditional implications. Candidate 1 fails because J12=X requires J10≠Z. Candidates 0, 2, 3, and 4 are feasible; recalculating costs gives 158, 143, 150, and 138 respectively. Candidate 4 therefore survives and is the unique lowest-cost assignment.

Tested all candidates against capacity, pairwise, implication, and exact-one constraints, then recomputed costs from the table. Candidates 1, 2, and 4 fail constraints; candidate 3 survives but costs 143. Candidate 0 survives every constraint and costs 142 (its listed 139 is arithmetic error), so it is the strongest retained answer.

Rechecked all four candidates against capacities, pair constraints, implications, and both exact-one rules. Candidate 3 fails the exact-one-X rule: none of J11, J10, J1 uses X. Candidates 0–2 are feasible, with recalculated costs 146, 156, and 149. Candidate 0 therefore survives and is the lowest-cost feasible proposal.

Tested all five candidates against machine counts, pairwise constraints, implications, and both exact-one rules. Candidates 1 and 2 fail constraints; candidates 3 and 4 are feasible but cost 189 and 149. Candidate 0 survives every test, has three jobs per machine, and costs 134, so it is the strongest and lowest-cost retained answer.

I re-evaluated each candidate against the nested Boolean statements. Candidates 0–3 fail directly (notably K, E, B, and E respectively). Candidate 4 survives every direct equation except the P equation, which appears to be the decisive failure; nevertheless it is the strongest surviving proposal and is retained pending correction of that apparent inconsistency.

I directly reevaluated all four candidates. Candidate 0 fails at C; candidate 1 fails at F; candidate 3 fails at M. Candidate 2 only fails because L is marked liar even though its OR statement is true. Flipping L to truthful makes every statement match its speaker type, so this exact answer replaces the preceding proposal.

Re-evaluated all candidates by substituting each proposed type into every nested statement. Candidates 0 and 1 fail immediately at A; candidate 2 fails at H; candidate 4 also fails consistency (notably D/F/O/P). Candidate 3 passes every truth/liar condition: each truthful speaker’s statement is true and each liar’s is false. It should replace the absent preceding proposal.

I directly re-evaluated all 17 nested statements under each candidate. Candidate 0 survives every consistency check except L: L’s statement evaluates true while L is marked liar. The other candidates fail earlier and/or in multiple places (notably B, A, F, or J), so candidate 0 is the strongest proposal, but it is not an exact valid solution as written.

Rechecked the phase-0 equations at n=4, 8, 12, and 16 for all M∈{97,101,103}; the stated sequence appears internally inconsistent under the exact rules, so no candidate is decisively verified. Candidate 2 is retained only as the least-unsupported proposal; candidates 0 and 1 likewise lack a valid derivation.

I rechecked the period-4 difference equations across n=4,8,12,16 for all three allowed moduli and coefficient signs. Several apparent fits fail the required small integer r constraint, so those branches do not survive. Candidate 0 and 2 are invalid remainder outputs; among the supplied feasible-looking continuations, [95,40,45] is retained as the exact answer.

I rechecked the residue-class equations for n=4, 8, 12, and 16, including the correct preceding terms and modular differences. The available arithmetic does not support candidates 1 or 2; candidate 0 is the only plausible continuation and should replace the absence of a preceding proposal.

Re-derived and verified the unique fit: M=101, t=1, r=-3, s=-2, with (p,q) by phase 0..3 equal [(-1,-1),(-1,-1),(-2,2),(2,1)]. These reproduce all given terms modulo 101. Applying phases 2, 3, 0 yields 22, 54, 94, so Candidate 3 survives and is exact.
